coredata.dc:title: The synthesis and characterization of carbon dots and their application in dye sensitized solar cell

Sharma, Vinay
Kang, Hyeonggon
Karim, Fahim

Mobin, Shaikh M.
Uddin, Jamal

Hossain, Faruque
Kabir, Humayun
Uddin, Nizam
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDOTs) are increasingly becoming popular in the areas ranging from sensing and bioimaging to electronics. The interesting optical properties of CDOTs make it vital to explore its potential in the development of sustainable energy. In this work, one-step hydrothermally synthesized CDOTs were used as sensitizing agent in the fabrication of dye sensitized solar cell. The fabrication of the CDOT-based dye sensitized solar cell and its performance characteristics are explored in depth. The fabricated dye sensitized solar cell performance in terms of efficiency, voltage, and current was evaluated using a standard illumination of air-mass 1.5 global (AM 1.5 G) having an irradiance of 100 mW/cm [2]. The photon-to-current conversion efficiency (η) of only the carbon dot sensitized solar cell was 0.10% whereas the efficiency of the solar cell fabricated with a sensitizing dye made up of CDOT and N719 was 0.19%. As compared with the performance DSSCs fabricated with only 719 dye, it was observed that when CDOT was used in combination with N719 as sensitizing dye, the open circuit voltage increases yet the overall efficiency of the resulting solar cells decreases. It is clear from the result that CDOT could be used as a sensitizing dye in DSSCs. However, it is not very useful when used in combination with other sensitizing dyes due to energy transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dye
DSSC
N719
Photoanode
Solar cell

The authors are also grateful to the Institution of Advancement, Coppin State University, for administrative help. ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CDOT and its use in dye sensitized solar cell.
Absorption spectra of CDOT and CDOT/(TiO2) N719.
Normalized Emission spectra of CDOT and CDOT/N719 measured in water. (Inset not normalized).
Fluorescence lifetime measurement of CDOT, N719 and CDOT/N719.
Size analysis of the carbon nanoparticles via dynamic light scattering measurements.
Energy dispersive spectra of carbon dots.

TEM of carbon dots and titanium dioxide nanoparticles. (a) Carbon dot sensitized titanium dioxide nanoparticles; (b) bare titanium dioxide nanoparticles; (c) carbon dots; (d) histogram of carbon dot size distribution.
FT-IR spectra of CDOT, CDOT/TiO2 and CDOT/N719/TiO2.

Photocurrent-voltage characteristics of CDOT, CDOT/N719 and N719 dye sensitized solar cell measured under illumination of 100 mW/cm2 (1.5 a.m.). (a) Comparing only CDOT and CDOT + N719. (b) Comparing CDOT, CDOT/N719 and N719.
Bode plot of CDOT, CDOT/N719 and N719 dye sensitized solar cells.
Nyquist plot of CDOT, CDOT/N719 and N719 dye sensitized solar cells.
Current voltage characteristics of CDOT DSSCs.

head.ce:title: The synthesis and characterization of carbon dots and their application in dye sensitized solar cell

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Discipline of Biosciences and Bio-Medical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDOTs) are increasingly becoming popular in the areas ranging from sensing and bioimaging to electronics. The interesting optical properties of CDOTs make it vital to explore its potential in the development of sustainable energy. In this work, one-step hydrothermally synthesized CDOTs were used as sensitizing agent in the fabrication of dye sensitized solar cell. The fabrication of the CDOT-based dye sensitized solar cell and its performance characteristics are explored in depth. The fabricated dye sensitized solar cell performance in terms of efficiency, voltage, and current was evaluated using a standard illumination of air-mass 1.5 global (AM 1.5 G) having an irradiance of 100 mW/cm [2]. The photon-to-current conversion efficiency (η) of only the carbon dot sensitized solar cell was 0.10% whereas the efficiency of the solar cell fabricated with a sensitizing dye made up of CDOT and N719 was 0.19%. As compared with the performance DSSCs fabricated with only 719 dye, it was observed that when CDOT was used in combination with N719 as sensitizing dye, the open circuit voltage increases yet the overall efficiency of the resulting solar cells decreases. It is clear from the result that CDOT could be used as a sensitizing dye in DSSCs. However, it is not very useful when used in combination with other sensitizing dyes due to energy transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, also referred to as carbon quantum dot are fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm. Depending on the conditions and materials employed, particles of different size range could be synthesized [1–4]. Carbon dots have unique characteristics that make them versatile for several applications such as bioimaging [5–8] and drug delivery [9–11]. Owing to their ability to bind to other materials possessing hydroxyl, carboxy and amino groups, carbon dots are viewed as useful materials capable of enhancing the photoanodic properties of dye sensitized solar cells. Dye sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are a type of solar cells that convert the sun's energy to electric energy using a sensitizing dye [12–14]. A typical DSSC is made up a photoanode, a counter electrode and a redox couple electrolyte sandwiched between the two electrodes [15–17]. The photoanode generally consist of a dye sensitized film of titanium dioxide nanoparticle on a conductive glass slide. In the presence of incident light, the dye molecules absorb photons and are excited. At the excited state, the electrons are released from the dye and injected into the conduction band of the titanium dioxide. The dye used in the fabrication of DSSC is the most important component of the solar cell. It is responsible for capturing photons of energy from the sun and initiating a process through which solar energy is converted to electric energy. Different types of dyes, both natural and synthetic dyes, have been explored for use as sensitizing dyes in dye sensitized solar cell [18–26]. Most of these dyes usually absorb in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum and are usually endowed with functional groups that allows for easy attachment of the dye to the titanium dioxide nanoparticles and consequently facilitate the efficient transfer or electron from the excited dye to the conduction band of the titanium dioxide [27]. DSSCs based on natural dyes though are environmentally friends usually have relatively low efficiencies. DSSCs fabricated using synthetic dyes on the other hand have relatively higher solar-to- electric energy conversion efficiency yet dyes frequently contain certain metals that are not environment friendly. Nanomaterials have certain characteristics that make them versatile for use in dye sensitized solar cell in a way that both improves their efficiency and does no damage to the environment [28,29]. CDOT is a fluorescent carbon material that has gained a lot of attention due to their unique photo-properties [30–32]. In this work, CDOT is used as a sensitizing agent for harvesting energy from the sun. The surface of CDOT are endowed with carboxyl moieties which for good interaction with titanium dioxide needed for charge transfer. CDOT also possess qualities such as good stability and conductivity that make them suitable as sensitizing agents for dye sensitized solar cells.

The CDOTs were synthesized by hydrothermal pyrolysis of sodium citrate and urea. The CDOTs were characterized in detail and employed as a sensitizing agent in dye sensitized solar cell. Fig. 1 shows schematic of the synthesis of carbon dots and its application in dye sensitized solar cells.

UV–Visible measurements were carried out to study the absorption characteristics of the CDOT and that of a mixture of the CDOT and N719 - a popular dye commonly used as a sensitizing dye in the fabrication of dye sensitized solar cells. The results of the absorption studies are displayed in Fig. 2 . The wavelength of maximum absorption of the CDOT in water was 340 nm. The peak at 340 nm corresponds to the pi–pi* transition. There is another absorption band (not displayed) at 233 nm corresponding to n–pi* transition. The UV–Vis of a mixture of CDOT and N719 was taken to observe the wavelength of absorption of the combined dyes so as to verify their capacity to absorb photons for the generation of electric power. The spectra for the mixture of the two dye show clearly defined peaks of each of the components. There is a slight red shift in absorption band of CDOT in the mixture. This shift is due the change in the surface of the CDOT after its interaction the N719.

The steady state fluorescence measurements were carried out to study the emission characteristics of CDOTs and that of the combined solution of CDOTs and N719 dye. The wavelength of excitation was 400 nm. The wavelength of maximum fluorescence for the CDOTs as seen in the spectra was 460 nm. The emission spectra of CDOT and that of CDOT/N719 are displayed in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence of the carbon dot is quenched or drastically reduced when mixed with the N719 dye.

The CDOTs were further characterized by means of fluorescence lifetime measurements. The fluorescence measurements are displayed in Fig. 4 and Table 1 . The lifetime of the CDOT was 2.34 ns and 7.83 s, and that of the CDOT/N719 was 1.36 and 6.19 ns and finally 33.9 ns, and 0.54 ns for N719 dye. The lifetime is consistent to earlier work on carbon dots [33–35]. The decrease in the fluorescence lifetime could be due to the quenching of fluorescence originating from the interferences of the two fluorescence bands of the CDOT and N719. This could also account for the difference in solar to electric power efficiencies of the DSSC fabricated using the respective dyes.

The carbon dots were characterized using dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements to determine the hydrodynamic diameter of the particles. Fig. 5 shows the DLS spectrum of the CDOTs. The average diameter of the particles were found to be 7.5 nm with a standard deviation of 1.2 nm. The size by DLS are usually bigger than the size determined via transmission electron microscopy and other imaging techniques since DLS takes into consideration the size of the core CDOTs as well as that of solvent molecules attached to the outside of the CDOTs.

Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) Measurements were carried out to study the composition of the carbon dot nanoparticles. Fig. 6 is a display of the EDS image of the carbon dots. The EDS gives the elemental composition of the carbon dot. The elements observed in the EDS measurement were Carbon, Oxygen and Sodium. The Sodium is attributed to residues of citrate still remaining in the sample after purification. In the synthesis of CDOT, sodium citrate dihydrate is utilized as the reducing agent.

The size of the CDOT was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy imaging. Fig. 7 shows a representative TEM image of the carbon dots attached to titanium dioxide (a), bare titanium dioxide (b) and bare carbon dot nanoparticles(c). Overall the particles are spherical in shape but there a number of them which are close to irregular in shape. Fig. 7d is a histogram of the carbon dot and their average size calculated from 140 carbon dot. The sizes of the particles varied from 2 nm to 10 nm with an average size of 5.49 nm and a standard deviation of ±1.15 nm. This size is consistent with that of the DLS which was 7.5 nm. The DLS gives the hydrodynamic diameter which is usually bigger than that of the size as determined by TEM.

The Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out for the samples used in the fabrication of the DSSCs so as to analyze the bonding in the CDOT molecule as well as its interaction with titanium dioxide and also with N719. Fig. 8 shows the FTIR spectra of CDOT sensitized titanium dioxide film, CDOT/N719 sensitized titanium dioxide film and bare CDOT. The spectrum with titanium dioxide mainly shows three vibrational modes which are attributed to TiO2 for υO-H at 3413 cm−1, δO-H at 1800 cm−1, and υTi-O at 476 cm−1 as a broad stretching. The FTIR spectra also show peaks at 3470 cm−1, 1580 cm−1, and 492 cm−1 which are respectively ascribed to the CO, NH, CC and CO bond in the CDOT particles. The peaks seen at 1400 cm−1 could be indication of a COC asymmetric stretch.

The photo conversion efficiency of the assembled DSSCs were determined by measuring the current and voltages of the fabricated CDOT-based dye sensitized solar cells. The measurement were performed with an illumination of air-mass 1.5 global (AM 1.5G) having an irradiance of 100 mW/cm2 which are standard conditions for dye sensitized solar cell measurements [36–38]. Fig. 9 shows the photocurrent-voltage curves of the cells. The solar to electric power efficiency of the CDOT dye sensitized solar cell was 0.10% with a fill factor of 0.22 and a short circuit current and open circuit voltage of 0.89 mA/cm2 and 0.49 V, respectively. The CDOT/N719 fabricated devices had a relatively higher efficiency stemming from a high open circuit voltage. It had a short circuit current of 0.89 mA/cm2 and an open circuit voltage of 0.89 V with a fill factor of 0.24. The efficiency was computed to be 0.19%. The efficiency of the N719 dye sensitized solar cells measured under the same condition was 1.35%. The current voltage characteristics of CDOT DSSCs has been summarized in Table 2 .

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out on the fabricated CDOT dye sensitized solar cells. EIS measurements are one of the most beneficial tools used to investigate the charge transfer and transport processes in dye-sensitized solar cells. [39,40] , The results of the EIS measurements are usually displayed in the form of Nyquist and Bode plots. Fig. 10 and Fig. 11 show the Bode and Nyquist plot, respectively.

This study reports on the synthesis and characterization of carbon dot and its application in the dye sensitized solar cells. The carbon dots were synthesized using hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and urea. It was then characterized for structural and optical properties and its potential to be used as a sensitizing agent. The photocurrent and photo voltage characteristics as well as the impedance measurements were also carried out under illumination of air-mass 1.5 global (AM 1.5 G) having an irradiance of 100 mW/cm [2]. The efficiency (η) of only the carbon dot sensitized solar cell was 0.10% while that of the N719 together carbon dot was 0.19%. While the short circuit current for both was 0.89 mA/cm2, the open circuit voltage of the CDOT/N719 was much higher (0.89 V) than that of CDOT DSSC (0.49 V) and thus a comparatively higher efficiency. It was observed that although CDOT has some sensitizing effect that ensure the capture of photons for the generation of electric power, it is unable to improve the sensitizing effect of the commonly used N719 dye. This observation could be attributed to the energy transfer that occurs between the N719 and the CDOT.

Materials used for the experiments included titanium dioxide powder (Degussa P-25) purchased from the Institute of Chemical Education, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Department of Chemistry, Madison, WI, USA. Graphite paste was purchased from Ted Pella, Inc., Redding, CA, USA. Fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glass slides were purchased from Harford glass company, Hartford City, Indiana, USA. Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH), isopropyl alcohol, (CH3)CH3CH2OH) acetone (C3H6O), ethanol (C2H5OH) and acetic acid (CH3CO2H) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MS, USA) and were used without further purification. Sodium citrate dihydrate and Urea were procured from Merck & Co. Kenilworth, NJ, USA.

The CDOTs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and urea. In a typical procedure, 3 g of sodium citrate dihydrate and 3 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL water and stirred for 10 min to make a homogenous mixture. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in a furnace at 180°C for 5 h. Further, the solution was left to cool down at room temperature and greenish black CDOT suspension was obtained. Sodium citrate and urea were earlier used for CDOT synthesis using microwave and electrolysis [41,42].

Size analysis of the carbon dots wasperformed with dynamic light scattering instrument (DLS) purchased from Horiba, Edison New Jersey, NJ, USA. Absorption spectroscopy was carried out with UV-3600 Plus from Shimadzu, MD, USA. Emission spectroscopy was measured with RF-5301PC from Shimadzu, MD, USA. Fluorescence lifetime measurement was carried out with Horiba lifetime instrument from Horiba, Edison New Jersey, NJ, USA. TiO2 paste was printed on FTO glass using WS-650 Series Spin Processor from Laurell Technologies Corporation, PA, USA. The cell performance was measured using 150 W fully reflective solar simulator with a standard illumination of air-mass 1.5 global (AM 1.5 G) having an irradiance of 100 mW/cm2 (Sciencetech Inc.), London, Ontario, Canada. Reference 600 Potentiostat/Galvanostat/ZRA from GAMRY Instruments (Warminster, PA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dye sensitized solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: The working electrode was prepared by first spin coating a thin layer of TiO2 on the conductive side of a fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) glass. The titanium dioxide coated FTO glass was subsequently annealed at 450 °C for an hour and then allowed to cool down to room temperature. To obtain the sensitized sample, the TiO2 coated FTO glass was immersed in carbon dots solution overnight. With the N719/CDOT photoanode, the N719 and CDOT were mixed together and the TiO2 coated FTO glass immersed in it overnight. Following this, the sample was rinsed and dried with ethanol and acetone, respectively. The counter electrode (cathode) was prepared by painting an FTO glass slide with a layer of colloidal graphite. The carbon dot sensitized photoanode and the carbon electrodes were assembled to form a solar cell by sandwiching a redox (I−/I3 −) electrolyte solution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was financially supported by the University of Maryland System Wilson E. Elkins Professorship, Constellation, an Exelon Company, E2- Energy to Educate grant program (163893), and Dept. of Education, SAFRA Title III Grant. The authors are also grateful to the Institution of Advancement, Coppin State University, for administrative help. The content is exclusively the responsibility of the authors and does not necessarily represent the official views of the funding agencies.
